perturbation.original_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”. NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR OTHERWISE, REGARDING ITS ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS OR PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the disclaimer of warranties related to the accuracy, completeness, or performance of confidential information could be problematic. Massachusetts law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, and disclaiming all warranties could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent this covenant if the information is knowingly inaccurate or incomplete.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract's performance or enforcement. Removing the warranty disclaimer may, under certain circumstances, contradict this implied covenant if one party provides knowingly inaccurate or incomplete confidential information, potentially acting in bad faith.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the warranty disclaimer in the changed text creates a legal contradiction. The original text explicitly disclaims warranties regarding the accuracy, completeness, or performance of confidential information. By removing this disclaimer, the changed text potentially exposes a party to liability for inaccuracies or incompleteness, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, could contradict the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts law if the party knowingly provides inaccurate information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that 'Every contract or duty within this chapter imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Removing the disclaimer could be seen as an attempt to circumvent this obligation if the confidential information provided is knowingly flawed, thereby contradicting the law's requirement of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall survive for a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall survive for a period determined at the disclosing party's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Massachusetts has statutes of limitations for breach of contract. Setting the term to be determined by one party may conflict with the statute of limitations if the disclosing party attempts to enforce the agreement beyond the statutory period or terminates the agreement prematurely, preventing recourse. This introduces legal uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law dictates that contract actions generally must be commenced within six years after the cause of action accrues. By allowing the disclosing party to arbitrarily determine the agreement's term, this conflicts with the statutory six-year limitation, potentially barring the receiving party from rightful claims if the agreement is prematurely terminated or enforced beyond a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part III, Title V, Chapter 260, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part III COURTS, JUDICIAL OFFICERS AND PROCEEDINGS IN CIVIL CASES Title V STATUTES OF FRAUDS AND LIMITATIONS Chapter 260 LIMITATION OF ACTIONS Section 2 Contract actions; actions upon judgments or decrees of courts of record Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Contract actions; actions upon judgments or decrees of courts of record Section 2. perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the disclosing party to determine the agreement's term, which contradicts the statute of limitations for contract actions in Massachusetts. Both scraped law snippets, specifically referencing Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 260, Section 2, state that contract actions must be commenced within six years after the cause of action accrues. By allowing one party to arbitrarily determine the agreement's term, this potentially circumvents the six-year limitation, preventing the receiving party from rightful claims if the agreement is prematurely terminated or enforced beyond a reasonable timeframe. This aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, as a legally mandated protection is omitted, making it non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, without reference to conflict of laws principles. This docu-ment contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction of the disclosing party's choosing. This docu-ment contains the agreement between the parties with respect to some of the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to be at the disclosing party's choosing and implying the agreement isn't the 'entire agreement' introduces ambiguity and could violate Massachusetts' principles of contract interpretation, particularly the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if the chosen jurisdiction is selected to unfairly disadvantage the other party. The omission of 'entire' can lead to disputes about unenumerated understandings.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the disclosing party to choose the governing law arbitrarily and suggesting the agreement is not the entire agreement, this change violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts law. Selecting a jurisdiction with laws favoring the disclosing party and implying other agreements exist demonstrate a lack of transparency and potentially unfair dealing.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the obligation of good faith as outlined in Massachusetts General Law Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article 1, Section 1-304. The original text specifies Massachusetts law as governing, ensuring a predictable legal framework. The change allows the disclosing party to choose the governing law, potentially selecting a jurisdiction that favors them, thus violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Additionally, the original text states the document contains the 'entire agreement,' while the changed text implies it covers 'some of the subject matter,' suggesting other agreements might exist, which also undermines transparency and good faith. This introduces ambiguity and the potential for unfair dealing, directly contradicting the legal requirement for good faith in contract performance and enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be adjudicated exclusively in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be subject to arbitration at the sole discretion of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: While arbitration is permissible, making it solely at the disclosing party's discretion can be deemed unconscionable, especially if the arbitration process is biased or inaccessible to the other party. This could violate Massachusetts' principles of fairness and good faith in contract enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: While M.G.L. c. 251, § 2(a) provides for the enforceability of arbitration agreements, making arbitration solely at the discretion of one party introduces an element of unconscionability. This clause could be challenged if the arbitration process is designed to unfairly favor the disclosing party, undermining the principle of mutual consent and fair dispute resolution implicit in Massachusetts contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part III, Title IV, Chapter 251, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part III COURTS, JUDICIAL OFFICERS AND PROCEEDINGS IN CIVIL CASES Title IV CERTAIN WRITS AND PROCEEDINGS IN SPECIAL CASES Chapter 251 UNIFORM ARBITRATION ACT FOR COMMERCIAL DISPUTES Section 2 Refusal to arbitrate; application to superior court Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Refusal to arbitrate; application to superior court Section 2. (a) A party aggrieved by the failure or refusal of another to proceed to arbitration under an agreement described in section one may apply to the superior court for an order directing the parties to proceed to arbitration. If the opposing party denies the existence of the agreement to arbitrate, the court shall proceed summarily to the determination of the issue so raised and shall, if it finds for the applicant, order arbitration; otherwise, the application shall be denied. (b) Upon application, the superior court may stay an arbitration proceeding commenced or threatened if it finds that there is no agreement to arbitrate. Such an issue, when in substantial and bona fide dispute, shall be forthwith and summarily determined, and if the court finds for the applicant it shall order a stay of arbitration; otherwise the court shall order the parties to proceed to arbitration. (c) If an issue referable to arbitration under the alleged agreement is involved in an action or proceeding pending in a court having jurisdiction to hear applications under paragraph (a), the application shall be made therein, otherwise and subject to section seventeen, the application may be made in any court of competent jurisdiction. (d) Any action or proceeding involving an issue subject to arbitration shall be stayed if an order for arbitration or an application therefor has been made under this section or, if the issue is severable, the stay may be with respect to such issue o

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates disputes be adjudicated in Massachusetts courts. The changed text allows arbitration at the sole discretion of one party (the disclosing party). This introduces a potential for unfairness. While M.G.L. c. 251, § 2(a) does provide for arbitration agreements, the law explanation points out that making arbitration solely at one party's discretion can be unconscionable if it unfairly favors that party. The scraped snippets (both 1 and 2) discuss the process for compelling or staying arbitration, but do not explicitly address the issue of one-sided discretion, suggesting the contract modification could be challenged on the basis of fairness and good faith, creating a legal contradiction. The discretion granted to only one party undermines the principle of mutual agreement, which is an Omission Legal Contradiction.
